blocks.text: Before You Enter the Exam Hall

Equipment: blue or black ink pen (required for all written answers). HB pencil (required for graphs, diagrams, and mechanism drawings). Eraser. Ruler (for graph drawing and reading). Cambridge-approved scientific calculator.
Sleep: 8 hours minimum. Precise chemical recall and calculation fluency both deteriorate significantly with poor sleep.
Light breakfast — sustained cognitive energy for 75 minutes of intensive analytical writing.

Arrive 15 minutes early. Mental warm-up: mentally recite 5 key definitions (first IE, electronegativity, Ka, activation energy, empirical formula). Do NOT read new content — this raises anxiety without benefit.
Write at the top of your answer booklet (before the exam starts): the three mole formulae, the calorimetry formula (q = mcΔT; ΔH = −q/n), and 'WHAT + WHY' — your reminder for every explain question.
Opening Protocol: The First 5 Minutes

rows: ✍️ STRUCTURED Q STRATEGY

STEP 1 (2 minutes): READ ALL QUESTIONS FIRST. Read every sub-part of every question before writing a single word. Identify: (a) which topics are being tested; (b) which questions you are most confident in; (c) any sub-parts that clearly build on earlier sub-parts.
STEP 2: For each sub-part, annotate the margin with the command word and mark allocation. Write 'WHAT+WHY' next to every 'explain' sub-part. Write 'SHOW ALL STEPS' next to every 'calculate' sub-part.
STEP 3: Begin with the question you are most confident in — not necessarily question 1. Building early marks creates positive momentum and calmer thinking for harder questions.
STEP 4: Work through all questions. NEVER leave a sub-part blank — write at minimum the most basic relevant chemical fact. A blank = 0 marks. A partial attempt may earn 1 mark.

blocks.text: In-Exam Discipline: The Non-Negotiable Rules

rows: Situation in the Exam
The Correct Response
You have written one long sentence for a 3-mark question
STOP. Count how many distinct chemical points you have made. If fewer than 3, add more specific points. One point restated three ways = 1 mark, not 3.
You cannot remember a specific definition
Write the most precise version you can recall. Include all components you remember. Never leave a definition blank — partial definitions earn partial marks.
Your calculation gives a negative value when you expect positive (or vice versa)

Do NOT adjust the sign to match expectations. Show your working. If the sign is genuinely wrong, trace back through your steps for a sign error. Write a sentence noting the sign: 'the negative sign indicates the reaction is exothermic'.
You make an error in part (a) and need it for part (b)
Continue using your wrong (a) answer in (b) — show this explicitly: 'Using the value from (a)...'. ECF marks are awarded for correct subsequent method even with a wrong starting value.
You run out of time on a question

Write the key phrase for each remaining mark — even a single sentence identifying the relevant principle. Never leave a sub-part completely blank in the last minutes. 1 mark per sub-part attempted is better than 0.
A mechanism question requires curly arrows
Use pencil. Draw the arrow starting FROM a bond or lone pair, pointing TO where the electrons move. Check: does each arrow start at electrons (bond or lone pair)?
An 'explain' question has 4 marks and you have only written 2 points
You need 2 more distinct chemical points. Ask: WHAT happens? WHY does it happen? What is the CONSEQUENCE? What is the EXCEPTION? Each is a potential additional mark.
You have 10 minutes remaining and 15 marks left to write
Triage: calculate the mark-yield per minute. Write at least the command-word-matching key phrase for each remaining sub-part. Half an answer earns more than no answer.

blocks.text: The A* Structured Question Mindset

The difference between a student who scores 48/60 and one who scores 56/60 is not knowledge — it is precision of expression. The student scoring 56/60 consistently does five things the student scoring 48/60 does not:

rows: 48/60 Student Behaviour
56/60 Student Behaviour
Writes 'the melting point is high because of the strong bonds'
Writes 'the melting point is high because a large amount of energy is required to overcome the strong electrostatic forces of attraction between oppositely charged ions in the giant ionic lattice'
Writes one long paragraph for a 4-mark explanation
Writes four distinct, numbered/separated sentences, each containing one marking point
In calculations: writes numbers without units
Writes units at every step: 'n = m/Mr = 5.85 g / 58.5 g mol⁻¹ = 0.100 mol'
In mechanism: draws curly arrows from atoms, not bonds/lone pairs
Always starts curly arrows from the bond or lone pair — checks this for every arrow before moving on
Leaves 3 sub-parts blank in the last 10 minutes
Writes at least one chemically relevant sentence per remaining sub-part — earning partial marks on every question attempted
✅ EXAMINER TIP

The marks for Paper 2 are already on the paper — your job is to extract them with precise language. Every sub-part has a mark scheme with specific marking points. Your answers do not need to use the exact same wording, but they must contain the same chemical content.
For every 'explain' question: check that your answer contains a 'because' or 'this is because'. If it does not, you have described without explaining. Add the reason immediately.

The data sheet is your friend — it contains bond energies, electrode potentials, NMR and IR tables. Refer to it for every relevant calculation. Do not rely on memory for specific numerical data when the sheet is provided.

blocks.text: Essential Definitions — Mark Scheme Language
These are the exact mark scheme formulations for the most frequently tested definitions in Paper 2. Learn them word-for-word.

rows: Term
Full Mark Scheme Definition
Key Components
First ionisation energy
The minimum energy required to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms in their ground state (to form one mole of gaseous 1+ ions).
minimum energy; one mole; gaseous atoms; ground state
Electronegativity
The power of an atom to attract electrons to itself in a covalent bond (Pauling definition).
power; atom; attract electrons; to itself; covalent bond
Bond energy
The energy required to break one mole of a particular covalent bond in the gaseous state (averaged over similar molecules).
one mole; particular bond; gaseous state; energy to break
Activation energy
The minimum energy required for a collision between reactant molecules to be effective (to result in the formation of products).
minimum energy; collision; effective; products
Dynamic equilibrium
A state in which the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the reverse reaction, and the concentrations of reactants and products remain constant (in a closed system).
forward = reverse rate; concentrations constant; closed system
Le Chatelier's principle
If a change is made to a system at dynamic equilibrium, the position of equilibrium shifts to oppose / minimise that change.
change; oppose/minimise; position of equilibrium
Ka
The equilibrium constant for the dissociation of one mole of a weak acid in aqueous solution. Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA].
equilibrium constant; dissociation; weak acid; expression
Buffer solution
A solution that resists changes in pH when small amounts of acid or alkali are added to it (contains a weak acid and its conjugate base, or a weak base and its conjugate acid).
resists pH change; small amounts of acid or alkali; weak acid + conjugate base
Empirical formula
The simplest whole number ratio of atoms of each element present in a compound.
simplest; whole number ratio; each element
Atom economy
Atom economy = (Mr of desired product / sum of Mr of all products) × 100%
desired product only in numerator; all products in denominator
